edges.description: Excess platelet-VWF binding removes hemostatically efficient VWF multimers from circulation.
edges.source: Hyperresponsive platelet GPIb-alpha binding to von Willebrand factor

edges.description: Removal of platelet-VWF complexes reduces circulating platelet counts.
Loss of hemostatically efficient VWF multimers contributes to mucosal and cutaneous bleeding.
Reduced platelet formation and increased platelet-VWF clearance reduce platelet counts.

edges.target: Hyperresponsive platelet GPIb-alpha binding to von Willebrand factor

evidence.snippet: This inherent platelet function defect is due to a gain-of-function mutation within the GP1BA gene coding for the platelet surface glycoprotein Ib alpha protein, the receptor for the adhesive protein von Willebrand factor (VWF).

By direct sequencing of GP1BA, a novel leucine-rich repeat heterozygous variant was identified (c.580C>T; predictably p.Leu194Phe), strongly suggestive as being the underlying cause for the PT-VWD phenotype of our patient.

nodes.description: GP1BA gain-of-function variants make platelet GPIb-alpha hyperresponsive to von Willebrand factor, producing excessive platelet-VWF interaction at the platelet surface.
nodes.id: Hyperresponsive platelet GPIb-alpha binding to von Willebrand factor

evidence.snippet: The defect results in excessive and unnecessary platelet-VWF interaction with subsequent removal of the hemostatically efficient high molecular weight VWF as well as platelets from the circulation, leading to thrombocytopenia and bleeding diathesis.

nodes.description: Experimental work in patient-derived and murine PT-VWD megakaryocytes shows early VWF binding, abnormal proplatelet formation, defective collagen-induced RhoA-MLC2 signaling, ectopic platelet release in bone marrow, and increased clearance of platelet-VWF complexes.

evidence.snippet: Platelet-type von Willebrand disease megakaryocytes bound vWF at an early differentiation stage and generated proplatelets with a decreased number of enlarged tips compared to control megakaryocytes.
Similarly, bone marrow from TgG233V mice showed an increased number of platelets compared to bone marrow from TgWT mice

nodes.description: Mild to moderate mucocutaneous bleeding that can worsen during pregnancy or after aspirin or other antiplatelet drug exposure.

evidence.snippet: Patients with PT-VWD present with mild to moderate mucocutaneous bleeding, which becomes more pronounced during pregnancy and following aspirin ingestion or drugs that have antiplatelet activity.

nodes.description: Platelet concentrates are preferred for significant bleeding or procedures because the primary defect is in the patient's platelet receptor rather than plasma VWF. VWF/FVIII preparations or desmopressin should not be assumed equivalent to standard VWD therapy because raising circulating VWF can worsen thrombocytopenia.

evidence.snippet: Treatment is based on making a correct diagnosis of PT-VWD where platelet concentrates instead of VWF/factor VIII preparations should be administered.
She was transfused two units of platelets before delivery.

nodes.description: Excess platelet-VWF binding removes hemostatically efficient high-molecular weight VWF multimers and platelet-VWF complexes from circulation, impairing primary hemostasis.

evidence.snippet: The defect results in excessive and unnecessary platelet-VWF interaction with subsequent removal of the hemostatically efficient high molecular weight VWF as well as platelets from the circulation, leading to thrombocytopenia and bleeding diathesis.

The defect results in excessive and unnecessary platelet-VWF interaction with subsequent removal of the hemostatically efficient high molecular weight VWF as well as platelets from the circulation, leading to thrombocytopenia and bleeding diathesis.

nodes.description: VWF/factor VIII preparations should not be used as the default replacement strategy for PT-VWD bleeding, because platelet concentrates address the platelet-receptor defect more directly.

evidence.snippet: Treatment is based on making a correct diagnosis of PT-VWD where platelet concentrates instead of VWF/factor VIII preparations should be administered.
